beats.text: A graph isn’t just a picture, every part gives us information to read.”

params.description: Graph from warm-up fades away and a new graph appears on screen
Horizontal picture graph appears showing Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3. Key visible: each fruit symbol = 1 vote.

beats.text: Let’s look at a new graph. A class of kids voted on their Favorite Fruits. Click on the fruit category that got the most votes.”
Click on the category that got the MOST votes.

validator.description: Student clicked Apples — correct, 6 votes is the highest value

beats.text: Right! The graph shows us that Apples had the most votes at 6.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the symbols of fruits in each row. Which one has the most?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: Nope, not quite. Count the symbols in each row. Each symbol is 1 vote. Which fruit has the most symbols on the graph?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System highlights Apples row and counts each symbol aloud: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6

beats.text: Let’s walk through step by step. I'll count the Apples row: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6. Now Bananas: 1, 2, 3, 4. Oranges: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5. Grapes: 1, 2, 3. The biggest number is 6.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Fruits data. Apples=6, Bananas=4, Oranges=5, Grapes=3. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values alongside the graph.

beats.text: You found the most. Now find the opposite. Which fruit got the LEAST votes?
Which fruit got the LEAST votes?

validator.description: Student clicked Grapes — correct, 3 votes is the lowest value

beats.text: Correct, Grapes got the least with only 3 votes. You can compare categories by reading each row.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Which row has the smallest number of symbols?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: Not exactly. Each symbol is 1 vote. Count each row: Apples 6, Bananas 4, Oranges 5, Grapes 3. Which number is smallest?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System highlights Grapes row and displays count of 3

beats.text: Let’s work through this. Each symbol is 1 vote so we count the symbols. Apples has 6, Bananas has 4, Oranges has 5, and Grapes has 3. Three is the smallest number, so Grapes got the least votes. You can compare categories by reading each row.

elements.description: Horizontal picture graph in reading mode. Favorite Fruits data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Fruits values.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears showing Animals at the Zoo data. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6. Key visible: each animal icon = 1 animal.
Data table appears alongside the graph, showing the same Animals at the Zoo values.

beats.text: Every part of a graph has a job. Watch.
The {title} tells us what the data is about: Animals at the Zoo.

params.description: Category labels highlight on the graph.

beats.text: The {labels} tell us what each category shows.

params.description: Table highlights and symbols in the graph highlight.

beats.text: The {key} shows the {scale}. Here, each animal picture equals 1 animal. That's a scale of 1.

params.description: The {table} shows the exact information we put in our picture graph

beats.text: Now you try. Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.
Click on the part that tells us what each symbol means.

validator.description: Student clicked the key — correct component

beats.text: Yes, that's the key. It shows the scale. Each symbol equals 1 animal.

validator.description: Student clicked wrong component on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look for the part that shows what each picture means.

validator.description: Student clicked wrong component on second attempt

beats.text: No, let's double check.  What part tells us each animal picture equals 1 animal?

validator.description: Student clicked wrong component three or more times — system models the answer

beats.text: Let’s look for this together. The key is this box that says each animal picture equals 1 animal. That's the scale and it tells us what each symbol means.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6. click_component tool active targeting key.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

beats.text: This picture graph is different than the Favorite Fruits graph you saw before. This one goes up and down. We call that {vertical}. Same idea. Use the key to read it. How many monkeys are at the zoo?
How many monkeys are at the zoo?

validator.description: Student selected 7 — correct, Monkeys value

beats.text: Exactly, 7 monkeys. Each symbol equals 1, so you count 7 symbols.

validator.description: Student selected 5 — counted Lions column instead of Monkeys

beats.text: That's the Lions value. Look for the Monkeys label at the bottom and count the symbols in that column.

validator.description: Student selected 6 — counted Penguins column instead of Monkeys

beats.text: That's the Penguins value. Look for the Monkeys label at the bottom and count the symbols in that column.

validator.description: Student selected 8 — miscounted or added incorrectly

beats.text: Not exactly. Let's count the Monkeys column carefully. Each symbol equals 1 animal. Count them one at a time.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System counts Monkeys symbols one by one from bottom to top: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7

beats.text: Let’s count together. Find the Monkeys label at the bottom. Each symbol is 1 monkey so count up: 1, 2, 3, 4, 5, 6, 7. The answer is 7 monkeys.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. multiple_choice tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

beats.text: Let's read one more value from this graph. Which animal has FEWER than 4 at the zoo?
Which animal has FEWER than 4 at the zoo? Click on it.

validator.description: Student clicked Elephants — correct, 3 is fewer than 4

beats.text: You got it, Elephants. There are only 3 elephants, which is fewer than 4.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Count the symbols in each column and look for the one with fewer than 4.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: Let's think about this together. Fewer means less than 4. Each symbol is 1 animal. Count each column's symbols. Which one has less than 4?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: System counts symbols in each column, highlighting Elephants with 3 symbols

beats.text: ”Let’s take a closer look. Fewer than 4 means less than 4. The numbers less than 4 are 3, 2, 1, 0. Lions have 5, Monkeys have 7, and Penguins have 6 which are all more than 4. Elephants have 3, which is the only animal lower than 4. Elephants is the answer.”

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

beats.text: You’re getting the hang of reading picture graphs. Now let's see another way to show data: bar graphs.

params.description: Picture graph transforms into a vertical bar graph with axis 0 to 8, same Animals at the Zoo data. Bars replace symbol columns.

beats.text: Same information, different format. Instead of counting symbols, we read where the bar ends.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph showing Animals at the Zoo data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s. Lions=5, Monkeys=7, Elephants=3, Penguins=6.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

beats.text: In a bar graph, the {height} of the bar tells us the amount. Look at the Monkeys bar. Click on it and find how tall the bar is. That tells us how many monkeys there are.
Click on the bar for Monkeys and find where the bar ends.

validator.description: Student clicked Monkeys bar — correct

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Monkeys bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 7 highlights on axis.

beats.text: Correct, 7 monkeys. Same as the picture graph. The bar goes up to 7.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Which bar is showing the value for Monkeys?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: Not exactly, we need the Monkeys bar. Read the categories along the bottom of the graph and click the bar labeled "Monkeys".

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Monkeys bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 7 highlights on axis.

beats.text: Let's look at how this is done together . We want to know the number of monkeys. I read the categories below the bars and find the one called "Monkeys". That's the bar to click on so we can follow the top of this bar over to see what number it reaches on the axis. It shows there are 7 monkeys. This is the same amount we found in the picture graph.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Animals at the Zoo data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Animals at the Zoo values.

params.description: Vertical bar graph appears showing Books Read This Week data. Axis 0 to 8 by 1s. Maya=4, Aiden=6, Sofia=5, James=7.

beats.text: Here's a new bar graph comparing the books read this week by 4 students. The scale is 1. Each mark on the side shows 1 book. How many books did Sofia read?
How many books did Sofia read?

validator.description: Student selected 5 — correct, Sofia's value

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Sofia's bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 5 highlights on axis.

beats.text: That’s right, Sofia read 5 books. You read that from seeing where her bar ends compared to the numbers on the axis.

validator.description: Student selected 4 — Maya's value instead of Sofia's

beats.text: Not exactly, 4 is Maya's value. The question asks about Sofia. Find Sofia and see where her bar stops on the axis.”

validator.description: Student selected 6 — Aiden's value instead of Sofia's

beats.text: Not quite, that's Aiden's value. We need to find how many books Sofia read. Find Sofia and see where her bar stops on the axis.

validator.description: Student selected 7 — James's value instead of Sofia's

beats.text: No, 7 is James's value. We need Sofia's bar. Find Sofia and see where her bar stops on the axis.”

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Horizontal line draws from top of Sofia's bar perpendicular to vertical axis. Value 5 highlights on axis.

beats.text: Let’s find this answer together. The question asks about Sofia's books. First, I find Sofia's name at the bottom. Then I follow her bar up until it ends and across to the axis. Her bar stops at 5, so Sofia read 5 books.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read This Week data. multiple_choice tool active.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.

beats.text: Graphs make comparing easy. You can see which is most and least at a glance by the height of the bars. Who read the FEWEST books this week? Click the category.
Who read the FEWEST books this week?

validator.description: Student clicked Maya — correct, 4 books is the lowest value

beats.text: Correct, Maya read the fewest books: 4 books. It's the shortest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Look at the height of each bar. Which one is the shortest?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: Not there yet. Fewest means the smallest number. Select the category with the shortest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: Maya's bar pulses and is highlighted as the shortest bar on the graph

beats.text: Let’s compare each category together. Look at all the bars: James' bar is tallest at 7, then Aiden's at 6, Sofia's at 5, and Maya's is shortest at 4. The shortest bar shows the fewest books, that's Maya. Maya read the fewest books this week.

elements.description: Vertical bar graph in reading mode. Books Read This Week data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Books Read This Week values.

params.description: Horizontal bar graph appears showing Favorite Colors data. Axis 0 to 10 by 1s labeled Number of Votes. Blue=8, Red=5, Green=4, Yellow=6.

beats.text: This bar graph goes sideways. We call that {horizontal}. The {length} of the bar tells us the amount. Which color on this graph is the most popular? Click on the longest bar.
Which color is the most popular? Click on the longest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked Blue — correct, 8 votes is the highest value

beats.text: Yes, Blue is the most popular. 8 students voted for blue. It has the longest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Most popular means the most votes. Which bar reaches farthest across?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: No, that's not it. The longest bar shows the most votes. Which bar goes all the way to 8?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: Guideline draws from end of Blue bar to axis, showing it reaches 8

beats.text: Let's take a closer look together. Blue's bar goes all the way to 8. That's longer than any other bar. Red goes to 5, Green to 4, Yellow to 6. The longest bar shows the most votes, so blue is the most popular color.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.

beats.text: Same graph, different question. Which color got the LEAST votes? Click on the bar.
Which color got the LEAST votes?

validator.description: Student clicked Green — correct, 4 votes is the lowest value

beats.text: Exactly, Green got the least. Only 4 votes. It's the shortest bar.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on first attempt

beats.text: Not quite. Which bar is the shortest?

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category on second attempt

beats.text: No, let's double check. The shortest bar shows the least votes. Find the bar that only reaches to 4.

validator.description: Student clicked a wrong category three or more times — system models the answer

params.description: Guideline draws from end of Green bar to axis, highlighting it reaches only 4

beats.text: Let’s think about this again. We're looking for the LEAST votes, the smallest number. Green's bar is the shortest. It only reaches 4 on the axis. That's less than Blue's 8, Red's 5, and Yellow's 6. Green got the least votes.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. click_category tool active.
Data table showing Favorite Colors values.

beats.text: You found the most and the fewest. Now let's answer some different kinds of questions.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph showing Favorite Colors. Data table no longer visible.

beats.text: How many more students chose blue than yellow?
“How many more” means we {compare} two amounts. We find the DIFFERENCE by subtracting.
Blue has 8 votes. Yellow has 6 votes. Since more students like blue, subtract Blue minus Yellow. How many more students chose blue than yellow?
How many more students chose blue than yellow?

validator.description: Student selected 2 — correct, 8 minus 6 equals 2

beats.text: Correct, 2 more students voted for blue than yellow. You found the difference: 8 minus 6 equals 2.

validator.description: Student selected 8 — the value of Blue instead of the difference

beats.text: Not exactly. 8 is how many chose blue. But the question asks how many more chose blue than yellow. We need to find the difference by subtracting.

validator.description: Student selected 14 — added Blue and Yellow instead of subtracting

beats.text: Not quite. How many more means we compare by subtracting, not adding. Blue has 8, Yellow has 6. Subtract to find the difference.

validator.description: Student selected 3 — counting error or misread the values

beats.text: Close, but check your subtraction carefully. Blue reaches 8, and Yellow reaches 6. Subtract to find how many more.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Animation shows Blue bar at 8 and Yellow bar at 6, then visually demonstrates the difference of 2 units between them

beats.text: Let's work through this together. Blue has 8 votes. Yellow has 6 votes. To find how many more chose blue, we subtract: 8 minus 6 equals 2. The answer is 2.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Blue and Yellow bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

beats.text: New question: How many fewer students chose red than blue?
”How many fewer” tells us to {compare}. We still find the difference.
How many fewer students chose red than blue?

validator.description: Student selected 3 — correct, 8 minus 5 equals 3

beats.text: Yes, 3 fewer students voted for red than blue. Blue has 8, Red has 5. The difference between 8 and 5 is 3.

validator.description: Student selected 5 — chose the value of Red instead of the difference

beats.text: Not quite. You found how many chose red. But the question asks how many FEWER chose red. That means we find the difference between Red and Blue.

validator.description: Student selected 8 — chose the value of Blue instead of the difference

beats.text: No, let's look closer. You found how many chose only blue. But how many fewer asks for the difference. That means subtract Red from Blue to compare them.

validator.description: Student selected 13 — added the values instead of finding the difference

beats.text: Not exactly. You added the votes for Red and Blue together. How many fewer means find the DIFFERENCE so subtract the smaller number from the bigger number.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Visual shows Blue bar at 8, Red bar at 5, with difference of 3 highlighted between them

beats.text: Walk through this with me. Blue has 8 votes, Red has 5 votes. To find how many fewer, we subtract: 8 minus 5 equals 3. Red has 3 fewer votes than blue.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Red and Blue bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

beats.text: Here's a different kind of question: How many students chose yellow or red IN ALL?
When a question asks about things IN ALL or TOGETHER, we {combine} them. We put the parts together to find the total. We ADD to {combine}.
How many students chose yellow or red IN ALL?

validator.description: Student selected 11 — correct, 6 plus 5 equals 11

beats.text: Correct, 11 students in all. Yellow has 6, Red has 5.

validator.description: Student selected 1 — likely confused about what 'in all' means or miscounted

beats.text: Not quite. In all means we want to combine to get the total. Find how many students chose Yellow and Red, then add those two numbers together.

validator.description: Student selected 6 — only counted Yellow, forgot to add Red

beats.text: Not exactly. You found Yellow's value, but the question asks for yellow or red in all. That means combine so add both values together.

validator.description: Student selected 23 — added all categories instead of just Yellow and Red

beats.text: Not quite. The question asks for only yellow or red, not all the colors. Find Yellow's value and Red's value, then add just those two.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Yellow bar shows 6, Red bar shows 5, visual addition 6+5=11 appears

beats.text: Here’s how we approach this. We want the total of yellow and red. Yellow has 6 votes. Red has 5 votes. 6 plus 5 equals 11 votes for those colors.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.

beats.text: Sometimes you need TWO steps to answer a question. Let's try one together.
Question: How many more students chose Yellow or Red together compared to Green?
Let's break this question into two parts. First, we once again combine Yellow and Red. Then we compare that number to Green.
Step 1: Determine how many chose yellow or red together.
How many chose yellow or red together?

validator.description: Student selected 11 — correct, 6 plus 5 equals 11

beats.text: Exactly, 11 in all. You combined Yellow’s 6 with Red’s 5.

params.description: Result from step 1 displayed: Yellow + Red = 11

validator.description: Student selected 5 — only counted Red, not both categories

beats.text: Not quite. You found Red's value. The question asks for Yellow and Red together. Add both values to find how many in all.

validator.description: Student selected 6 — only counted Yellow, not both categories

beats.text: Not quite. You found Yellow's value. The question asks for Yellow and Red together. Add the value from both bars to find how many in all.

validator.description: Student selected 14 — added Yellow, Red, and Green instead of just Yellow and Red

beats.text: Almost, you added too many categories. Combine only Yellow and Red. Green comes in the next step when we compare.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Yellow bar (6) and Red bar (5) pulse in sequence, then total 11 appears

beats.text: Let me show you how this works. Yellow has 6 votes. Red has 5 votes. When we combine them, we add them together. 6 plus 5 equals 11. That's how many chose Yellow or Red in all.

elements.description: Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Yellow and Red bars highlighted. multiple_choice tool active.
Horizontal bar graph in reading mode. Favorite Colors data. Green bar highlighted. Result from step 1 displayed. multiple_choice tool active.
Result from step 1: Yellow + Red = 11

beats.text: Step 2: Now compare that total to Green. How many more is 11 than the votes for Green?
How many more is 11 than the votes for Green?

validator.description: Student selected 7 — correct, 11 minus 4 equals 7

beats.text: Yes, 7 more students like Red or Yellow compared to Green. You used TWO steps:  first you {combined} to find a total, then you {compared} by finding the difference.

validator.description: Student selected 4 — gave Green's value instead of the difference

beats.text: Nope, not quite. You found only Green's value. The question asks how many more 11 is than Green's 4. Subtract to compare.

validator.description: Student selected 11 — gave the total from step 1 instead of the difference

beats.text: Not there yet. That's the total from step 1. Now compare it to Green's 4. How many more is 11 than 4? Subtract to find the difference.

validator.description: Student selected 15 — added 11 and 4 instead of subtracting

beats.text: Not quite. The question asks how many more, not how many in all. When you compare two amounts, SUBTRACT the smaller from the larger.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: Green bar (4) extends to match against the total 11, showing difference of 7

beats.text: Let’s work through the second step together. We have 11 from step 1. Green has 4 votes. To find how many more 11 is than 4, we subtract: 11 minus 4 equals 7. That's the difference. 7 more students chose Yellow or Red together compared to Green.
You used TWO steps to answer that question:  first you {combined} to find a total, then you {compared} by finding the difference.

params.description: Vertical picture graph appears. Pets We Have data. Dogs=7, Cats=9, Fish=4, Birds=6, Lizards=1. Key: each X = 1 pet.

beats.text: Look at this graph. It shows how many pets each student in a class owns. The next question will ask: How many fewer students have fish than dogs? Before we solve it, which categories do you need?
Which categories do you need? Select all that apply.
Now solve it. Remember: “how many fewer” means compare or find the difference. How many fewer students have fish than dogs?
How many fewer students have fish than dogs?

validator.description: Student selected 3 — correct, 7 minus 4 equals 3

beats.text: Exactly, 3 fewer students. Each symbol represents 1 pet. 7 students have dogs and 4 students have fish. The difference is 3.

validator.description: Student selected 4 — the value for Fish instead of the difference

params.description: Visual comparison showing Dogs (7) and Fish (4) with difference highlighted

beats.text: Not quite. You counted the students that have fish. The question asks how many fewer have fish than dogs. That means find the difference between the two numbers.

validator.description: Student selected 7 — the value for Dogs instead of the difference

params.description: Visual comparison showing Dogs (7) and Fish (4) with difference highlighted

beats.text: Not quite. You counted the students that have dogs. How many fewer means compare. Subtract the smaller number from the larger number to find the difference.

validator.description: Student selected 11 — added 7 and 4 instead of subtracting

params.description: Visual comparison showing Dogs (7) and Fish (4) with difference highlighted

beats.text: No, that's not it: you added the students with fish and dogs. How many fewer means find the difference. That means to subtract, not add.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System demonstrates subtracting 4 from 7 to show difference of 3

beats.text: Let’s work on this together. Dogs have 7 symbols and Fish have 4 symbols. How many fewer means compare or find the difference so we subtract: 7 minus 4 equals 3. The answer is 3 fewer students have fish than dogs as pets.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. Dogs and Fish highlighted, others dimmed. multiple_choice tool active.

beats.text: Question: How many more students have cats than fish and lizards combined?
First, combine Fish and Lizards. How many students have fish or lizards at home?
How many students have fish or lizards combined?

validator.description: Student selected 5 — correct, 4 plus 1 equals 5

beats.text: Correct, 5 students in all. 4 have fish. 1 has lizards. 4 plus 1 equals 5.

params.description: Highlight Fish and Lizards on picture_graph_pets
Result from step 1 displayed: Fish + Lizards = 5

validator.description: Student selected 3 — counted only Fish, ignoring Lizards

beats.text: Not exactly. You subtracted Fish and Lizards. Combine means to add them together.

validator.description: Student selected 4 — counted only Fish column

beats.text: Nope, not quite. That's just Fish. The question asks for Fish and Lizards combined. Add both categories together.

validator.description: Student selected 6 — likely counted Birds instead of the correct categories

beats.text: Not quite. Look at which categories the question asks about for this step. We need Fish and Lizards combined first. Add those two together.

validator.description: Student selected 13 — added all categories instead of just Fish and Lizards

beats.text: No, that's not it. You added all the categories. This part of the question only asks for Fish and Lizards. Combine just those two.

validator.description: Student answered incorrectly — system models the correct answer

params.description: System highlights Fish and Lizards columns, counts symbols in each: 4 Fish, 1 Lizard, shows 4 + 1 = 5

beats.text: Let's tackle this one together. The question asks for Fish and Lizards combined. Each symbol represents 1 pet. First I count Fish: 4 symbols. Then I count Lizards: 1 symbol. Now I add them: 4 plus 1 equals 5. The answer is 5 students altogether have fish or lizards.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. multiple_choice tool active.
Vertical picture graph in reading mode. Pets We Have data. Cats highlighted. Result from step 1 displayed. multiple_choice tool active.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5

beats.text: Next, how many more students have cats compared to fish and lizards together?
How many more have Cats than the total of Fish and Lizards?

validator.description: Student selected 4 — correct, 9 minus 5 equals 4

beats.text: Yes, 4 more students have cats than fish or lizards at home. You combined first , then compared. You used TWO steps to answer questions about the pets students have at home.

validator.description: Student selected 5 — gave the sum from step 1 instead of finding the difference

beats.text: No, that's only the total from step 1. Now we need to compare 5 Fish and Lizards to the number of students with Cats. Find the number for Cats and then find the difference to compare.

validator.description: Student selected 9 — gave the Cats total instead of finding how many more

beats.text: Let’s think this second step through together. We found that 5 students have fish or lizards. Now we need how many more have cats. Cats has 9 symbols. The difference is 9 minus 5, which equals 4.
You combined first , then compared. You used TWO steps to answer questions about the pets students have at home.

You can read picture graphs and bar graphs. You know "how many more" and "how many fewer" mean to {compare}, subtract to find the difference. You know "in all" means {combine}, add to find the total. Let's see what you know.

elements.description: Vertical picture graph showing Pets We Have data.
Result from step 1: Fish + Lizards = 5
